sections.markdown:
- 5 km easy recovery run
- Keep this genuinely light and unambitious.
- Only run if Thursday leaves both ankles feeling better, not just tolerable.
- If there is still meaningful ankle feedback, replace this with full rest rather than forcing the distance.

- This landed on the planned Friday, `2026-04-17`, but it did not stay as the prescribed `5 km` recovery check. The completed run covered `10.030 km` in `1:00:23` moving time (`1:01:00` elapsed) with `111.8 m` of climbing, average HR `153.1`, and max HR `169`, so it finished `5.030 km` over plan.
- The stream data says the effort stayed controlled rather than turning into disguised quality work. Estimated first-half moving pace was `6:00 /km` and second-half pace was `6:02 /km`, while distance-split HR only rose from `151.7 bpm` to `154.4 bpm`. Apple Health matched the same workout at `10.030 km` and `3660` seconds with average HR `152.9` and max HR `169`, so the provider data agrees that this was one continuous easy-to-moderate run, not a bad linkage.
- What changed was cost, not execution style. Doubling the planned distance the day after Thursday's `7.293 km` run and two days after Wednesday's `21.27 km` half-marathon substitute turned a recovery slot into another meaningful aerobic load.

- The immediate read is better than the first post-run caution. Weekly running volume is now `45.717 km` through four runs, and this `10.030 km` run stayed controlled enough in both pace and HR drift that it does not look like a hidden overreach by itself.
- Because you also reported feeling really good after the run, the weekend no longer needs an automatic clampdown. Saturday can stay available as a true `4 km` shakeout if the ankles remain quiet and the same freshness is still there, Sunday can stay at the full planned `10 km` easy, and basketball remains the first thing to cut because court load adds fatigue without helping the week's running objective.

- Long term, this still helps the block because it shows you can cover `10 km` at a controlled aerobic effort on a week that already included a `21.27 km` substitute and another `7.293 km` support run. That is a useful durability signal for the half-marathon and marathon build.
- The risk is the same one already sitting over this block: easy and recovery days keep drifting into medium-cost days. A `10.030 km` run at `153.1 bpm` average HR is manageable only if the following sessions get cheaper, not if the week keeps accumulating extra load. The durable win here is protecting the separation between real recovery and real work so higher-volume weeks stay repeatable instead of becoming a chain of almost-hard days.
- Honestly felt really good after that run.

- Strava moving pace averaged `6:01 /km` across `10.030 km` in `1:00:23` moving time.
- Estimated first-half moving pace was `6:00 /km` and second-half pace was `6:02 /km` (+2 s/km), so pace stayed broadly even.

- Strava summary HR came in at `153.1 bpm` average and `169 bpm` max.
- Distance-split HR averaged `151.7 bpm` in the first half and `154.4 bpm` in the second (+2.7 bpm), which reads as broadly stable across the run.

- Moving time was `1:00:23` out of `1:01:00` elapsed (99.0%), which kept stoppage cost negligible.
- The downsampled moving stream shows `8` stop segments and `0.8%` stationary samples, so the run was mostly continuous with a few interruptions.

- Strava logged about `116.0 m` of climbing, with altitude moving between roughly `42.4 m` and `64.8 m`.
- Estimated climbing was `54.2 m` in the first half and `61.8 m` in the second, so the climbing load was split fairly evenly.
